title: UserInterface Object
description: Provides access to the user-interface related objects and functionality.

methods.name: classType
methods.description: Static function that all classes support that returns the type of the class as a string. The returned string matches the string returned by the objectType property. For example if you have a reference to an object and you want to check if it's a SketchLine you can use myObject.objectType == fusion.SketchLine.classType().

methods.name: createCloudFileDialog
methods.description: Creates a new CloudFileDialog object which provides the ability to show a file selection dialog to the user that allows them to choose a file from Fusion web client.

methods.name: createFileDialog
methods.description: Creates a new FileDialog object which provides the ability to show a standard file selection dialog to the user.

methods.name: createFolderDialog
methods.description: Creates a new FolderDialog object which provides the ability to show a standard folder selection dialog to the user.

methods.name: createProgressDialog
methods.description: Creates a new ProgressDialog object that you can use to display and control a progress dialog.

methods.name: getText
methods.description: Get the localized text for a specific application text string. The strings used by Fusion are stored in localized XML files that are installed with Fusion. On Windows, you can find them here:   %LocalAppData%\Autodesk\webdeploy\production\VERSION_CODE\StringTable   And on Mac, you can find them here:   ~/Library/Application Support/Autodesk/webdeploy/production/VERSION_CODE/Autodesk Fusion.app/Contents/Libraries/Neutron/StringTable   There is a folder for each language that Fusion supports, and the strings for that language are defined in files within that folder. Fusion will use the language specified by the user in their preferences.

methods.name: inputBox
methods.description: Displays a modal dialog to get string input from the user.

methods.name: messageBox
methods.description: Display a modal message box with the provided text.

methods.name: selectEntity
methods.description: Supports the selection of a single entity. This provides a simple way to prompt the user for for a selection in a script. If you need more control over the selection a command should be created and a SelectionCommandInput used.

methods.name: terminateActiveCommand
methods.description: Method that causes the currently active (running) command to be terminated

methods.name: toolbarPanelsByProductType
methods.description: Gets all of the toolbar panels associated with the specified product.

methods.name: toolbarTabsByProductType
methods.description: Gets all of the toolbar tabs associated with the specified product.

methods.name: workspacesByProductType
methods.description: Returns all of the workspaces associated with the specified product.

properties.name: activeCommand
properties.description: Gets the id of the command definition from the active command (the one that is currently running)

properties.name: activeSelections
properties.description: Gets the current set of selected objects.

properties.name: activeToolbar
properties.description: Retrieve the active Toolbar being displayed in the user interface.

properties.name: activeToolbarTab
properties.description: Retrieve the active ToolbarTab being displayed in the user interface. This may be null.

properties.name: activeWorkspace
properties.description: Gets the active workspace. The active workspace is the one currently active in the user interface. This can be null if there is no active product.

properties.name: allToolbarPanels
properties.description: Gets all of the toolbar panels. This returns all of the panels available, regardless of which workspace or product they're associated with.

properties.name: allToolbarTabs
properties.description: Gets all of the toolbar tabs. This returns all of the tabs available, regardless of which workspace or product they're associated with.

properties.name: commandDefinitions
properties.description: Gets all of the command definitions currently defined. This is all command definitions both internal and those defined through the API.

properties.name: isTabbedToolbarUI
properties.description: Returns true if Tabbed Toolbars are being used.

properties.name: isUIEnabled
properties.description: Gets and sets if the Fusion user interface is enabled or not. By default it is enabled allowing the user to interact with Fusion. When set to false, the UI is disabled which blocks all interaction, including running commands, manipulating the view and interacting with the browser.

properties.name: isValid
properties.description: Indicates if this object is still valid, i.e. hasn't been deleted or some other action done to invalidate the reference.

properties.name: objectType
properties.description: This property is supported by all objects in the API and returns a string that contains the full name (namespace::objecttype) describing the type of the object.   It's often useful to use this in combination with the classType method to see if an object is a certain type. For example: if obj.objectType == adsk.core.Point3D.classType():

properties.name: palettes
properties.description: Returns the collection object that provides access to all of the existing palettes and provides the functionality to create new custom palettes.

properties.name: progressBar
properties.description: Gets the ProgressBar object that can be used to display a progress bar in the lower-right corner of the Fusion window.

properties.name: statusMessage
properties.description: Gets and sets the current message displayed in the lower-right corner of the Fusion window. This is useful when displaying progress information to the user for the current process. Set the value to an empty string to remove the message. The lifetime of your message is indeterminant because Fusion uses the same field to display messages.   If your process is running in the main thread of Fusion, you will need to call adsk.doEvents to give control back to Fusion, so it can update the UI.   The ProgressBar can also be used as a way to communicate to the user the current progress of a running process.

properties.name: toolbars
properties.description: Gets a collection that provides access to the toolbars. This includes the left and right QAT, and the Navbar.

properties.name: workspaces
properties.description: Gets all of the workspaces currently available.

events.name: activeSelectionChanged
events.description: This event fires whenever the contents of the active selection changes. This occurs as the user selects or unselects entities while using the Fusion Select command. The Select command is the default command that is always running if no other command is active. Pressing Escape terminates the currently active command and starts the Select command. If the Select command is running and you press Escape, it terminates the current Select command and starts a new one.   This event is only associated with the selection associated with the Select command and does not fire when any other command is running. The event fires when there is any change to the active selection, including when the selection is cleared when the Select command is terminated. It is also fired when the user clicks in an open area of the canvas to clear the current selection.

events.name: commandCreated
events.description: The commandCreated event fires immediately after the command is created.

events.name: commandStarting
events.description: The commandStarting event fires when a request for a command to be executed has been received but before the command is executed. Through this event, it's possible to cancel the command from being executed.

events.name: commandTerminated
events.description: Gets an event that is fired when a command is terminated.

events.name: markingMenuDisplaying
events.description: The markingMenuDisplaying event fires just before the marking menu and context menus are displayed. The marking menu is the round menu displayed when the user right-clicks the mouse within Fusion. The context menu is the vertical menu displayed. The event provides both the marking menu and the context menu so you can examine and edit the contents of either one or both of them before they are displayed. Fusion will then display the marking and context menu that you've customized. If either one is empty it will not be displayed.

events.name: workspaceActivated
events.description: The workspaceActivated event fires at the VERY end of a workspace being activated. The client can add or remove WorkspaceEventHandlers from the WorkspaceEvent.

events.name: workspaceDeactivated
events.description: The workspaceDeactivated event fires at the VERY end of a workspace being deactivated. The client can add or remove WorkspaceEventHandlers from the WorkspaceEvent.

events.name: workspacePreActivate
events.description: The workspacePreActivate event fires at the VERY start of a workspace being activated. The client can add or remove WorkspaceEventHandlers from the WorkspaceEvent.

events.name: workspacePreDeactivate
events.description: The workspacePreDeactivate event fires at the VERY start of a workspace being deactivated. The client can add or remove WorkspaceEventHandlers from the WorkspaceEvent.

related_samples.name: Palette Sample
related_samples.description:
Demonstrates how to create a palette, how to dock and snap palettes and how Fusion communicates with the palette HTML. The sample is an add-in.  To use it, create a new Python add-in and replace the code with the code below. You also need to create an html file using the name and code below. The html file needs to be in the same folder as the py file.
	When you load the add-in, you'll see two new commands under the ADD-INS panel of the TOOLS tab. The "Show Custom Palette" command will cause the custom palette to be displayed. It will remain displayed until you click its Close button. Clicking the "Click to send info to Fusion" button on the palette, will send information to your add-in, which uses the API to display that information in a message box. Running the "Send Info to HTML" command to send data to the javascript running in the palette, which uses it to update the content of a paragraph.

palette.html

related_samples.name: Customizing the UI using the API Sample
related_samples.description: Demonstrates how to work with tabs, panels, and command in the user interface. The full source for C++ and Python samples can be downloaded. This is especially useful for getting the resource files.

related_samples.name: Write user interface to a file API Sample
related_samples.description: Writes out the full structure of the Fusion user interface. This information is useful when editing the user-interface, as discussed in the usre manual topic User-Interface Customization with Fusion's API

related_samples.name: Command Inputs API Sample
related_samples.description:
Creates a command dialog that demonstrates all of the available command inputs.

To use the sample, create a new Python or C++ script and copy and paste this code, replacing the default code. You also need to unpack this zip file which contains a resource folder into the same folder where the source code file (.py or .cpp) is.

related_samples.name: Custom Event Sample
related_samples.description: Demonstrates the ability to call into the main thread from a worker thread. This sample is an add-in. To use it, use the Scripts and Add-Ins command to create a new add-in. Delete all of the code in the newly created add-in and replace it with the code below. Have a model open that has a parameter named "Length". Load the add-in. The add-in will change the value of the parameter every two seconds using a random value between 1 and 10.

activeSelectionChanged.title: UserInterface.activeSelectionChanged Event
activeSelectionChanged.description: This event fires whenever the contents of the active selection changes. This occurs as the user selects or unselects entities while using the Fusion Select command. The Select command is the default command that is always running if no other command is active. Pressing Escape terminates the currently active command and starts the Select command. If the Select command is running and you press Escape, it terminates the current Select command and starts a new one. This event is only associated with the selection associated with the Select command and does not fire when any other command is running. The event fires when there is any change to the active selection, including when the selection is cleared when the Select command is terminated. It is also fired when the user clicks in an open area of the canvas to clear the current selection.

commandCreated.title: UserInterface.commandCreated Event
commandCreated.description: The commandCreated event fires immediately after the command is created.

commandStarting.title: UserInterface.commandStarting Event
commandStarting.description: The commandStarting event fires when a request for a command to be executed has been received but before the command is executed. Through this event, it's possible to cancel the command from being executed.

commandTerminated.title: UserInterface.commandTerminated Event
commandTerminated.description: Gets an event that is fired when a command is terminated.

markingMenuDisplaying.title: UserInterface.markingMenuDisplaying Event
markingMenuDisplaying.description: The markingMenuDisplaying event fires just before the marking menu and context menus are displayed. The marking menu is the round menu displayed when the user right-clicks the mouse within Fusion. The context menu is the vertical menu displayed. The event provides both the marking menu and the context menu so you can examine and edit the contents of either one or both of them before they are displayed. Fusion will then display the marking and context menu that you've customized. If either one is empty it will not be displayed.

related_samples.name: Marking Menu API Sample
related_samples.description: Demonstrates how to customize marking menu and context menu. This sample is an add-in. To use it, create a new add-in using the "Scrips and Add-Ins" command. Use any name you would like for the add-in. In the folder where the add-in was created edit the add-in name.py file and replace it's entire contents with the sample code below. You can also delete all the other files that were created for the add-in except for add-in name.manifiest. Start the add-in from the "Scripts and Add-Ins" dialog. Now, with the add-in running, whenever you right-click in the Fusion window, you'll get an entirely customized context menu. The default marking menu has been modified by the add-in by removing the existing commands and adding some custom commands.

workspaceActivated.title: UserInterface.workspaceActivated Event
workspaceActivated.description: The workspaceActivated event fires at the VERY end of a workspace being activated. The client can add or remove WorkspaceEventHandlers from the WorkspaceEvent.

workspaceDeactivated.title: UserInterface.workspaceDeactivated Event

workspacePreActivate.title: UserInterface.workspacePreActivate Event
workspacePreActivate.description: The workspacePreActivate event fires at the VERY start of a workspace being activated. The client can add or remove WorkspaceEventHandlers from the WorkspaceEvent.

workspacePreDeactivate.title: UserInterface.workspacePreDeactivate Event
workspacePreDeactivate.description: The workspacePreDeactivate event fires at the VERY start of a workspace being deactivated. The client can add or remove WorkspaceEventHandlers from the WorkspaceEvent.

classType.title: UserInterface.classType Method
classType.description: Static function that all classes support that returns the type of the class as a string. The returned string matches the string returned by the objectType property. For example if you have a reference to an object and you want to check if it's a SketchLine you can use myObject.objectType == fusion.SketchLine.classType().

syntax.python: returnValue = adsk.core.UserInterface.classType()
syntax.cpp:
#include 

returnValue = adsk::core::UserInterface::classType();

classType.return_description: Returns a string indicating the type of the object.

getText.title: UserInterface.getText Method
getText.description: Get the localized text for a specific application text string. The strings used by Fusion are stored in localized XML files that are installed with Fusion. On Windows, you can find them here: %LocalAppData%\Autodesk\webdeploy\production\VERSION_CODE\StringTable And on Mac, you can find them here: ~/Library/Application Support/Autodesk/webdeploy/production/VERSION_CODE/Autodesk Fusion.app/Contents/Libraries/Neutron/StringTable There is a folder for each language that Fusion supports, and the strings for that language are defined in files within that folder. Fusion will use the language specified by the user in their preferences.

syntax.python: returnValue = userInterface_var.getText(module, id, defaultValue)
syntax.cpp:
#include 

returnValue = userInterface_var->getText(module, id, defaultValue);

getText.return_description: The localized string or the defaultValue if one is not found.

parameters.name: module
parameters.description: The module name. This is the same as the StringTable .xml filename without the .xml extension and without the version number. For example, the file NaFusionUI10.xml contains many of the strings used for Fusion's modeling commands. When specifying the module, this is specified as "NaFusionUI".

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The id of the text. This is the same as the 'commandName' field in the StringTable .xml file.

parameters.name: defaultValue
parameters.description: A default string value to return if the module or string id is not found in the current locale.

inputBox.title: UserInterface.inputBox Method
inputBox.description: Displays a modal dialog to get string input from the user.

syntax.python:
# Uses no optional arguments.
(returnValue, cancelled) = userInterface_var.inputBox(prompt)

# Uses optional arguments.
(returnValue, cancelled) = userInterface_var.inputBox(prompt, title, defaultValue)

inputBox.return_description: Returns the string entered by the user but because the user can click Cancel, the canceled argument should be tested before using the string.

parameters.name: prompt
parameters.description: The message text to display in the dialog.

parameters.name: cancelled
parameters.description: Indicates if the dialog was canceled.

parameters.name: title
parameters.description: Sets the title for the dialog if specified, otherwise the script or add-in name is used.   This is an optional argument whose default value is "".

parameters.name: defaultValue
parameters.description: The default string that's shown when the dialog is initially displayed, otherwise the input box is empty.   This is an optional argument whose default value is "".

related_samples.name: Use inputBox to get value and evaluateExpression to validate it
related_samples.description: Uses the UserInterface.inputBox function to get a string from the user and then validates that the strinng entered is a valid expression by using the UnitsManager.evaluateExpression function.

messageBox.title: UserInterface.messageBox Method
messageBox.description: Display a modal message box with the provided text.

syntax.python:
# Uses no optional arguments.
returnValue = userInterface_var.messageBox(text)

# Uses optional arguments.
returnValue = userInterface_var.messageBox(text, title, buttons, icon)

messageBox.return_description: The button pressed to dismiss the dialog is returned.

parameters.name: text
parameters.description: The message text to display in the dialog.

parameters.name: title
parameters.description: If the optional title argument is provided, it sets the title for the dialog, otherwise the script or add-in name is used.   This is an optional argument whose default value is "".

parameters.name: buttons
parameters.description: The optional buttons array can be used to specify which buttons to display on the dialog. The first button provided is the default action. If buttons are not specified, the dialog will default to a single OK button.   This is an optional argument whose default value is MessageBoxButtonTypes.OKButtonType.

parameters.name: icon
parameters.description: The optional icon argument can be used to specify which icon to display, otherwise the default of no icon is used.   This is an optional argument whose default value is MessageBoxIconTypes.NoIconIconType.

selectEntity.title: UserInterface.selectEntity Method
selectEntity.description: Supports the selection of a single entity. This provides a simple way to prompt the user for for a selection in a script. If you need more control over the selection a command should be created and a SelectionCommandInput used. Remarks

syntax.python: returnValue = userInterface_var.selectEntity(prompt, filter)
syntax.cpp:
#include 

returnValue = userInterface_var->selectEntity(prompt, filter);

selectEntity.return_description: Returns a Selection object that provides access the selected entity through it's "entity" property along with the location in space where the entity was selected. Asserts if the selection is aborted.

parameters.name: prompt
parameters.description: The prompt displayed to the user during the selection.

parameters.name: filter
parameters.description: A string defining the types of entities valid for selection. The valid list of selection filters can be found here: Selection Filters. You can combine multiple types by using a comma delimiter. For example, the string "PlanarFaces,ConstructionPlanes" will allow the selection of either a planar face or a construction plane.

terminateActiveCommand.title: UserInterface.terminateActiveCommand Method
terminateActiveCommand.description: Method that causes the currently active (running) command to be terminated Remarks

syntax.python: returnValue = userInterface_var.terminateActiveCommand()
syntax.cpp:
#include 

returnValue = userInterface_var->terminateActiveCommand();

terminateActiveCommand.return_description: Returns true if terminating the active command was successful.

toolbarPanelsByProductType.title: UserInterface.toolbarPanelsByProductType Method
toolbarPanelsByProductType.description: Gets all of the toolbar panels associated with the specified product.

syntax.python: returnValue = userInterface_var.toolbarPanelsByProductType(productType)
syntax.cpp:
#include 

returnValue = userInterface_var->toolbarPanelsByProductType(productType);

toolbarPanelsByProductType.return_description: Returns a list of the toolbars associated with the specified product.

parameters.name: productType
parameters.description: The name of the product that you want the associated workspaces for. The full list of available products can be obtained by using the Application.supportedProductTypes property.

toolbarTabsByProductType.title: UserInterface.toolbarTabsByProductType Method
toolbarTabsByProductType.description: Gets all of the toolbar tabs associated with the specified product.

syntax.python: returnValue = userInterface_var.toolbarTabsByProductType(productType)
syntax.cpp:
#include 

returnValue = userInterface_var->toolbarTabsByProductType(productType);

toolbarTabsByProductType.return_description: Returns a list of the tabs associated with the specified product.

parameters.name: productType
parameters.description: The name of the product that you want the associated tabs for. The full list of available products can be obtained by using the Application.supportedProductTypes property.

workspacesByProductType.title: UserInterface.workspacesByProductType Method
workspacesByProductType.description: Returns all of the workspaces associated with the specified product.

syntax.python: returnValue = userInterface_var.workspacesByProductType(productType)
syntax.cpp:
#include 

returnValue = userInterface_var->workspacesByProductType(productType);

workspacesByProductType.return_description: Returns a list of the associated work spaces.

parameters.name: productType
parameters.description: The name of the product that you want the associated workspaces for. The full list of available products can be obtained by using the Application.supportedProductTypes property.
